My practical advice would be to give as much notice as possible, ideally more than your contract requires, to make the transition as smooth as you can and to leave a positive impression — because the working world is smaller than it seems and reputations travel. Be honest and professional in your resignation: you do not need to over-explain, but a brief, gracious note acknowledging the opportunity you were given goes a long way. Accept that some employers will be disappointed — that is unavoidable — but a company that invested three months in you has also benefitted from three months of your work. Move forward without shame, and honor the new opportunity by bringing your best from day one.

vocab.definition: an initial period of employment during which both sides evaluate whether the arrangement works
vocab.example: During the probation period, either the employee or the employer can end the arrangement with minimal notice.

vocab.definition: based on clear logic and reason rather than emotion
vocab.example: Taking the better offer was a rational decision, even if it felt uncomfortable in the moment.

vocab.definition: the formal act of leaving a job by giving notice to your employer
vocab.example: She submitted a professional resignation letter thanking the company for the opportunity and offering two weeks' notice.

vocab.definition: polite, kind, and considerate, especially in a difficult situation
vocab.example: His gracious resignation letter left a positive impression even though he was leaving after only three months.

vocab.definition: a close match between your personal goals and what a role or organization offers
vocab.example: The new role had strong alignment with her long-term career goals, which made the decision easier to justify.

vocab.definition: the idea that how you behave professionally becomes known in your industry over time
vocab.example: In a small industry, reputations travel fast — leaving professionally matters even when the circumstances are difficult.

title: Networking — Feeling Uncomfortable
question: Your friend says: "Everyone tells me I need to network to advance my career, but I'm very introverted and I find networking events extremely uncomfortable. I don't know what to say to strangers and I always end up leaving early. Any advice?"
answer: Networking does not have to look like a room full of strangers exchanging business cards — and for introverts, the traditional networking event is actually one of the least effective formats anyway. The most sustainable form of networking for someone like you is one-on-one connection: reach out directly to one person per week whose work you genuinely admire, and ask for a 20-minute virtual coffee to learn about their experience — most people are flattered by that kind of thoughtful, focused interest and happy to say yes. LinkedIn is also an incredibly powerful tool for introverts because it lets you connect and build relationships at your own pace, through written messages and content, without the pressure of real-time conversation. If you do attend events, set yourself a specific, achievable goal — such as having one meaningful conversation with one person — rather than trying to work the entire room, because depth always outweighs volume in relationship-building. Over time, as you build a network of genuine professional friendships rather than transactional contacts, the discomfort fades because the conversations start to feel natural. Networking is simply relationship-building, and introverts are often better at it than they think, because they listen deeply and make people feel genuinely heard.

vocab.definition: preferring quieter, less socially stimulating environments and finding large social events draining
vocab.example: As an introverted person, she found one-on-one coffee chats far more energizing than crowded networking events.

vocab.definition: able to be maintained comfortably over a long period of time
vocab.example: Reaching out to one new contact per week was a sustainable networking habit that he could maintain consistently.

vocab.definition: pleased and complimented by someone's positive attention or interest
vocab.example: She was flattered when a junior colleague asked for a coffee chat to learn from her experience.

vocab.definition: connections maintained only for immediate personal benefit, without genuine relationship
vocab.example: He moved away from collecting transactional contacts and focused instead on building a smaller, more genuine network.

vocab.definition: the idea that a few meaningful connections are more valuable than many shallow ones
vocab.example: In networking, depth outweighs volume — three strong relationships will open more doors than thirty weak ones.

vocab.definition: a live, immediate exchange of words, as opposed to written or delayed communication
vocab.example: For introverts, the pressure of real-time conversation at events can be reduced by preparing a few topics in advance.

title: Employee Stealing Credit for Work
question: Your friend says: "My manager presented my entire project to senior leadership without mentioning my name once. Everyone congratulated him, not me. I feel angry and invisible. Should I speak up, and if so, how?"
answer: Your anger is completely valid — having your work presented without attribution to senior leadership is a serious professional issue, not a minor oversight, and you absolutely should speak up about it. The approach I would recommend is a private, calm conversation with your manager rather than making it public or confrontational — request a one-on-one meeting and say something like "I was proud of the work on that project and I'd love to make sure senior leadership is aware of my contribution — how do we make that happen?" This gives your manager a face-saving opportunity to correct the situation, perhaps by following up with leadership directly or ensuring your name is attached to the next presentation. If your manager responds dismissively or defensively, then you have important information: this is a pattern of behavior and not a one-time mistake. In that case, consider how you might get visibility in front of senior leaders through other channels — presenting your own work in meetings, contributing to cross-functional discussions, or building a relationship with a senior mentor who knows your contributions directly. Document what you have done going forward so there is always a paper trail. You have earned the recognition you are seeking — do not let it disappear quietly.

vocab.definition: giving proper credit or recognition to the person responsible for something
vocab.example: She insisted on clear attribution in all reports so that each team member's contributions were properly acknowledged.

vocab.definition: allowing someone to avoid embarrassment while correcting a mistake
vocab.example: Framing the request as a question rather than an accusation gave her manager a face-saving way to fix the situation.

vocab.definition: treating something as unimportant or not worth addressing
vocab.example: When he raised the issue, his manager responded dismissively, which told him the problem was unlikely to be resolved internally.

vocab.definition: a written record of events or communications that can serve as evidence
vocab.example: She emailed her project updates to her manager every week to maintain a clear paper trail of her contributions.

vocab.definition: involving people from different teams or departments working together
vocab.example: Volunteering for cross-functional projects gave her direct exposure to senior leaders outside her own department.

vocab.definition: being seen and known by influential people in your organization
vocab.example: Presenting at the all-hands meeting gave him the visibility he needed to be considered for the director role.

title: Retiring Early vs. Continuing to Work
question: Your friend says: "I'm 55 and I have enough savings to retire early, but I'm not sure I'm ready to stop working. My family wants me to retire and travel with them, but I love my job and feel I still have a lot to contribute. What would you advise?"
answer: The fact that you love your work and feel you still have contributions to make is not a small thing — purpose, structure, and intellectual engagement are deeply linked to wellbeing in later life, and many people who retire earlier than they were ready to report feeling a surprising loss of identity. My advice would be to separate two decisions that are getting blended together: "Can I afford to retire?" and "Do I want to retire?" — because having the financial ability to stop does not mean that stopping is the right choice for your happiness right now. One middle path worth considering is a gradual transition: discuss with your employer whether a reduced schedule, a consulting arrangement, or a phased retirement over two to three years might be possible — this lets you reclaim time with your family while staying engaged in work you find meaningful. If travel is your family's priority, perhaps the answer is taking longer, more meaningful trips during holidays or sabbaticals rather than a full stop. Have an honest conversation with your family about what retirement actually means to you and what you fear losing — their support will mean far more once they understand how central your work is to your sense of self. There is no single right answer here, only the one that honors both who you are and who you love.

vocab.definition: the mental stimulation that comes from challenging and meaningful work or activity
vocab.example: Retirement without intellectual engagement left him restless within six months, so he returned to consulting.

vocab.definition: a gradual reduction in working hours or responsibilities before fully retiring
vocab.example: Her employer agreed to a phased retirement over two years, allowing her to transition without an abrupt stop.

vocab.definition: a period of paid or unpaid leave from work for rest, travel, or personal development
vocab.example: He took a three-month sabbatical to travel with his family without permanently stepping away from his career.

vocab.definition: a person's understanding of who they are, including their values, roles, and identity
vocab.example: Losing her job unexpectedly shook her sense of self in a way she hadn't anticipated.

vocab.definition: a flexible agreement to provide professional advice or services on a part-time or project basis
vocab.example: After retiring from her full-time role, she negotiated a consulting arrangement that kept her involved two days a week.

vocab.definition: a reason for doing something that gives life meaning and direction
vocab.example: Research consistently shows that having a sense of purpose is one of the strongest predictors of wellbeing in retirement.

title: Whistleblowing — Reporting Wrongdoing at Work
question: Your friend says: "I discovered that my company is doing something illegal — falsifying reports. I have evidence but I'm afraid to report it because I might lose my job and face retaliation. I also feel like I can't just stay quiet. What should I do?"
answer: You are right that you cannot simply stay quiet — witnessing illegal activity and saying nothing makes you complicit, and that weight will follow you long after you leave this company. Your fear of retaliation is completely legitimate, and the good news is that Canadian law specifically protects whistleblowers in many sectors from being fired or penalized for reporting illegal activity in good faith. My strongest advice before you do anything is to consult a lawyer — many employment lawyers offer a free initial consultation — because understanding your legal rights and the specific protections available to you in your province will make you far more confident and safe in whatever action you take next. While you are doing that, secure your evidence: save copies in a personal location that is not connected to your company's systems, and document everything with dates and details. In Canada, you can report to a regulatory body, a government agency, or in some cases directly to law enforcement, depending on the nature of the illegal activity. Reporting through official channels, rather than informally or publicly, provides you with the strongest legal protection. Doing the right thing carries risk, but it also carries the kind of integrity that defines your character long after this situation is resolved.

vocab.definition: involved in or partly responsible for wrongdoing by not acting to stop it
vocab.example: By staying silent about the fraud, she felt complicit — even though she had not participated directly.

vocab.definition: harmful action taken against someone for reporting wrongdoing or speaking out
vocab.example: Canadian law prohibits employers from taking retaliation against employees who report illegal activity in good faith.

vocab.definition: a person who reports illegal or unethical behavior within an organization
vocab.example: The whistleblower provided regulators with internal documents that proved the company had been falsifying safety reports.

vocab.definition: with honest and sincere intentions, without deception or malicious motive
vocab.example: He reported the issue in good faith, with no intention other than stopping the illegal activity.

vocab.definition: a government or independent authority that oversees a specific industry or sector
vocab.example: She reported the financial irregularities to the appropriate regulatory body rather than going to the media.

vocab.definition: the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
vocab.example: Choosing to report the wrongdoing — despite the personal risk — demonstrated the kind of integrity that defined his character.

title: Being Asked to Do Something Unethical
question: Your friend says: "My manager has asked me to alter some numbers in a report to make the results look better to clients. It's not technically illegal but it feels dishonest. I don't want to do it but I also don't want to lose my job. What advice would you give me?"
answer: The discomfort you are feeling is your moral compass working correctly — and the fact that something is "not technically illegal" does not make it ethical, and it does not protect you from the professional and personal consequences if the deception is ever discovered. My first piece of advice is to not do it, but to handle the refusal thoughtfully: rather than a flat no that puts you in immediate conflict with your manager, try asking clarifying questions — "Can we discuss how we're presenting this data? I want to make sure we're representing things in a way that will hold up if the client asks follow-up questions." This gives your manager an opportunity to reconsider without losing face, and it documents that you raised concerns. If pressure continues, you can state your position clearly and professionally: "I'm not comfortable altering the figures — I'd prefer we find another way to frame the results." If your manager or the company culture continues to push you toward dishonesty, that is important information about the kind of organization you are working for, and protecting your job security is not worth your professional integrity or your reputation. Quietly exploring other opportunities is wise — you deserve a workplace where honest work is valued.

vocab.definition: an internal sense of right and wrong that guides your decisions
vocab.example: Her moral compass wouldn't allow her to sign off on a report she knew contained misleading figures.

vocab.definition: the act of making someone believe something that is not true
vocab.example: Even if the deception seemed minor, it could have serious legal consequences if the client discovered the altered data.

vocab.definition: suffering a loss of dignity or respect in front of others
vocab.example: By framing his refusal as a concern rather than a challenge, he gave his manager a way to back down without losing face.

vocab.definition: to present information in a specific way to shape how it is understood
vocab.example: There is an ethical way to frame the results favorably without altering the underlying data.

vocab.definition: the commitment to ethical behavior and honesty in your work life
vocab.example: She turned down the promotion because accepting it would have required compromising her professional integrity.

vocab.definition: questions asked to better understand a situation before responding or acting
vocab.example: Asking clarifying questions gave him time to think and gave his manager a chance to reconsider the request.

title: Long Commute — Is It Worth It?
question: Your friend says: "I got a great job but the commute is three hours a day by public transit. After two months I'm exhausted, I'm not sleeping enough, and I feel like I spend my whole life on the bus. Is there a way to make this work, or should I look for something closer to home?"
answer: Three hours of daily commuting is genuinely significant — that is 15 hours per week, or roughly the equivalent of two full workdays spent in transit every single week — and the physical and mental toll you are describing after just two months suggests this pace is not sustainable long-term. Before deciding to leave, I would encourage you to explore whether the job itself has any flexibility: even two or three days working from home per week would cut your commute time nearly in half, and many employers are open to this conversation after an employee has demonstrated their value. If remote options are not available, there are ways to make the commute more productive and bearable — using the time for learning through podcasts or audiobooks, reading, or even relaxing with music — but I want to be honest: reclaiming that time, not just tolerating it, is what will matter long-term. Look also at whether moving closer to work is financially feasible — in Canada, rent and housing vary significantly by neighborhood, and sometimes the math works better than you expect. If after exploring all of this the commute is still unsustainable, there is no shame in acknowledging that a job you cannot physically sustain is not a great job for you, regardless of how good it looks on paper. Your health and energy are not optional.

vocab.definition: the negative effect something has on a person's health or wellbeing over time
vocab.example: The daily three-hour commute was taking a serious toll on his sleep, his mood, and his productivity.

vocab.definition: able to be maintained over time without causing serious harm
vocab.example: A three-hour daily commute is rarely sustainable beyond a few months before it affects health and performance.

vocab.definition: the ability to adapt working arrangements to different needs or circumstances
vocab.example: She asked about flexibility in her work schedule after demonstrating strong performance in her first few months.

vocab.definition: possible and practical to achieve given the circumstances
vocab.example: Moving closer to work became feasible once she calculated how much she was spending on transit each month.

vocab.definition: able to be tolerated or endured, even if not enjoyable
vocab.example: Listening to audiobooks made the long commute more bearable, though it never made it truly sustainable.

vocab.definition: taking back something that was previously lost or taken away
vocab.example: Working from home two days a week meant reclaiming six hours that had previously been lost to commuting.
